principles.label: Seamless Integration with the Human Body
principles.summary: Electronics designed for human health must be soft, stretchable, and conformable to seamlessly merge with the curved, dynamic nature of the human body.
principles.details: Traditional rigid and brittle electronics cannot properly interface with soft tissue over large areas without causing damage or missing distributed signals. The future lies in devices we can wear, attach, or implant seamlessly.
principles.representative_quote: Today’s electronics are rigid and brittle. We envision a future where the electronics are merged into what we wear and what we attach to our bodies or what we implant inside our bodies.

principles.label: No Compromise on Electronic Performance
principles.summary: To create truly functional stretchable electronics, materials must be designed from the molecular level to maintain high charge-carrier mobility even when mechanically elongated.
principles.details: Engineers must design polymer molecules that possess skin-like mechanical properties (stretchability, self-healing) while conducting electricity as effectively as metals or silicon. This often requires novel approaches like nano-confinement.
principles.representative_quote: we had to come up with new ideas for designing semiconductors so that we do not compromise one property for another—basically to have high charge-carrier mobility while still being able to reversibly elongate the material by at least twice its original length.

principles.label: Shift to Precision Health
principles.summary: Healthcare must shift from reactive Precision Medicine to proactive Precision Health through continuous, quantitative monitoring of physiological biomarkers.
principles.details: Instead of relying on subjective self-reporting or waiting for a disease to be discovered, continuous monitoring of biomarkers (like cortisol or heart rate variability) allows for the discovery of potential issues before they become catastrophic.
principles.representative_quote: there's a big need to move Beyond Precision medicine which is to come up with personalized treatment when a disease is discovered but to move towards Precision Health which is to monitor our house continuously and be able to discover a potential issue before that happens

principles.label: Nature as a Blueprint for Mechanical Resilience
principles.summary: Look to biological systems, such as human skin, to solve mechanical degradation and engineering bottlenecks through mechanisms like self-healing and weak molecular interactions.
principles.details: In nature, weak interactions between molecules lead to highly organized structures that perform specific jobs. Applying this principle to synthetic materials via dynamic bonds yields tunable, self-healing, and stretchable materials.
principles.representative_quote: When faced with the dilemma of mechanical fractures in biological systems, nature offers a smart solution: self-healing.

principles.label: Enhance, Don't Just Mimic
principles.summary: Artificial electronic skin should not only replicate human biological functions but also transcend them by integrating superhuman capabilities.
principles.details: Because electronic skin is artificially constructed, it presents an opportunity to integrate functionalities that biology naturally lacks, such as sensing distant heat, detecting specific chemicals, or reading an entire Braille sentence in a single touch.
principles.representative_quote: this is just the beginning of thinking about building electronic skin not only to mimic human function but also to enhance human function

principles.label: Align Projects with Long-Term Vision
principles.summary: New research ideas and projects should always align with a broader 5-to-10-year purpose or vision.
principles.details: Goals are short-term targets, but a purpose provides the fundamental reason for pursuing research. Having a long-term goal provides perspective to focus a research group's efforts and ensures exciting new ideas contribute to the overarching mission.
principles.representative_quote: We usually start a research project with a new idea that we think will have a significant impact, but at the same time this idea should align well with our overall vision of where we want our laboratory to be in the next five to ten years.

principles.label: Simultaneous Co-development of Materials and Fabrication
principles.summary: Hardware innovation requires interdependent development across materials, circuit design, and fabrication processes, ensuring compatibility with existing manufacturing infrastructure.
principles.details: Building complex stretchable circuits cannot be done by isolating variables. By intentionally developing materials that utilize existing fabrication tools (like those used for display screens), new technologies can scale to commercial manufacturing much more easily.
principles.representative_quote: We not only needed to develop new materials, but we also needed to develop the circuit design and the fabrication process to make the circuits. There are many layers stacked together and if one layer doesn’t work, we have to restart everything from scratch.

principles.label: Prioritize Fundamental Science Over Commercialization
principles.summary: Students and researchers should prioritize fundamental scientific training and discovery over the ambition to start a company.
principles.details: A solid fundamental understanding is necessary to develop the skills needed for solving complex problems. True innovation naturally brings tech transfer opportunities without needing to force them.
principles.representative_quote: As a student, your priority should be to learn, grow and obtain your degree. Regardless of what you choose to do in the future, first you need to be the best scientist or engineer you can.

frameworks.label: Skin-Inspired Material Design
frameworks.summary: A framework for designing new classes of flexible, biocompatible electronics by using human skin as the ultimate blueprint.
frameworks.details: Steps: 1) Identify a biological system with desired properties (e.g., human skin). 2) Define the specific properties to mimic (stretchability, biodegradability, self-healing). 3) Design molecular structures that replicate these macroscopic properties without losing electronic function. 4) Ensure output signals match biological receptors.
frameworks.representative_quote: So we decided to use human skin as the inspiration to define the properties and functions for the electronics we would design and build.

frameworks.label: Molecular Design for Contradictory Properties
frameworks.summary: An approach to engineering materials that require traditionally opposing characteristics, such as electrical conductivity and mechanical stretchability.
frameworks.details: Identify opposing requirements (e.g., rigid molecules for conduction vs. flexible molecules for stretching). Design a composite molecular structure that incorporates both elements (e.g., conductive polymers surrounded by ring-like shape molecules). Engineer the structure to dissipate mechanical energy without breaking the functional pathway.
frameworks.representative_quote: we design special molecules that have this ring-like shape molecules surrounding them so that when we pull these molecules these are circular like molecules can slide around and that's how we try to design these materials to be able to tolerate mechanical stretching and dissipate the mechanical energy without breaking

frameworks.label: Reframing Experimental Failure
frameworks.summary: A method for turning unexpected experimental results into creative breakthroughs by questioning foundational assumptions.
frameworks.details: When an experiment does not yield expected results, resist the urge to declare it a 'failure'. Instead, examine the original hypothesis to see if it relied on incorrect assumptions. Use the uncovered assumptions to develop creative new ideas that wouldn't have been considered otherwise.
frameworks.representative_quote: ...when an experiment does not work the way we initially thought, instead of declaring it a “failed” experiment, I encourage my students to think about whether our hypothesis may have incorrect assumptions.

frameworks.label: Spiral Transformation for Bioelectronic Fibers
frameworks.summary: A fabrication approach to integrate high-density microelectronic components into one-dimensional, soft, and curved fiber structures.
frameworks.details: Fabricate devices onto a 2D thin film using conventional microfabrication methods (like photolithography). Apply a 'spiral transformation' to physically convert the 2D thin film into a 1D soft fiber, precisely controlling the positioning of functional components to achieve a high-density layout.
frameworks.representative_quote: Here we use 'spiral transformation' to convert two-dimensional thin films containing microfabricated devices into one-dimensional soft fibres.

frameworks.label: Elastiff Layer Design (Strain Engineering)
frameworks.summary: A structural design method to protect active electronic components from mechanical deformation in intrinsically stretchable electronics.
frameworks.details: Ensure the overall material maintains high stretchability. Use a material for the 'elastiff' layer with a significantly higher Young's modulus than the substrate to minimize deformation in active regions. Ensure strong interfacial adhesion between the elastiff layer and the substrate to prevent delamination.
frameworks.representative_quote: The elastiff layers needed to fulfil three criteria. First, the material should have high stretchability... Second, the Young’s modulus... should be significantly higher than that of the substrate... Third, there must be strong adhesion of the elastiff layer with the substrate.

mental_models.label: Electronic Skin
mental_models.summary: Viewing human skin as the ultimate blueprint for next-generation electronics, combining physical properties (soft, stretchable, self-healing) with active neurological interfaces.
mental_models.details: It involves creating large-area, soft, stretchable sheets containing distributed sensors and processing circuits that can intimately interface with the body without causing damage, translating touch into electrical pulses to communicate with the nervous system.
mental_models.representative_quote: what we envision is to have electronics that are comfortable invisible imperceptible but compatible and be able to measure information autonomously so that's the electronic skin that we are trying to build

mental_models.label: Dynamic Bonds as Energy Shock Absorbers
mental_models.summary: Viewing weak, reversible chemical bonds as sacrificial links that break under mechanical strain to dissipate energy, protecting the primary covalent backbone.
mental_models.details: Instead of breaking permanent covalent bonds under stress, dynamic bonds (like hydrogen or metal-ligand bonds) break and then reconnect, allowing the material to heal and return to its original state without permanent damage.
mental_models.representative_quote: If covalent bonds are broken when the polymers are stretched, they cannot readily be reconnected at room temperature, so the material is permanently damaged. But if dynamic bonds are broken during stretching, they provide a place to use up the mechanical energy instead of breaking the covalent bonds.

mental_models.label: The Airplane Engine Analogy for Health
mental_models.summary: Comparing human health monitoring to airplane engine maintenance to advocate for continuous, proactive diagnostics.
mental_models.details: Just as engineers continuously monitor airplane engines to prevent catastrophic failures, we should continuously monitor human health to catch issues before they become severe diseases.
mental_models.representative_quote: imagine the engines for airplane we want to discover an issue before a catastrophic failure happens so that's exactly what we want to do for human health

mental_models.label: Nano-confinement Effect
mental_models.summary: A structural design approach where active electronic materials are confined at the nanoscale within a secondary polymer matrix to simultaneously achieve high electronic performance and mechanical stretchability.
mental_models.details: By blending a semiconducting polymer with a surface-energy-matched insulating polymer, the semiconductor is forced into planar conformations that improve charge transport, while the insulating matrix provides mechanical stretchability.
mental_models.representative_quote: in this Nano confined structure actually the polymer is forced to into more planar conformation and to reduce the confirmation or disorder and this is a key to result in much improved charge transport

mental_models.label: Beyond-Biomimicry
mental_models.summary: A lens for engineering where mimicking biology is only the baseline; once the artificial version is built, it serves as a platform to integrate capabilities that biology naturally lacks.
mental_models.details: For example, creating electronic skin that feels and looks human, but can also sense chemical environments or detect heat from far away without physical contact.
mental_models.representative_quote: since we're building this artificial um uh sheet or electronic sheet then uh it gives us the possibility to to add additional functionalities that our human scheme may not have

mental_models.label: Mechanical Heterogeneity
mental_models.summary: Introducing patterned regions of varying stiffness within a single substrate to selectively route mechanical strain away from fragile active components.
mental_models.details: By actively programming strain distribution, devices can maintain stable operation and high data quality when worn on the skin, even under significant global deformation.

heuristics.label: Look to Biology for Bottlenecks
heuristics.summary: When facing a fundamental engineering bottleneck, look to biological systems to see how nature solved the problem.
heuristics.representative_quote: When faced with the dilemma of mechanical fractures in biological systems, nature offers a smart solution: self-healing.

heuristics.label: Check for Incorrect Assumptions
heuristics.summary: Instead of calling an experiment a failure, check if your hypothesis had incorrect assumptions.
heuristics.representative_quote: ...when an experiment does not work the way we initially thought, instead of declaring it a “failed” experiment, I encourage my students to think about whether our hypothesis may have incorrect assumptions.

heuristics.label: Maintain 5-to-10-Year Goals
heuristics.summary: Have 5-to-10-year goals to maintain perspective and focus your group's efforts when evaluating new ideas.
heuristics.representative_quote: We usually start a research project with a new idea that we are very excited about, but at the same time this idea or direction has to align with our overall vision and match up with where we want to be in the next five or ten years.

heuristics.label: Match Surface Energy in Polymer Blends
heuristics.summary: When blending polymers for stretchable electronics, match the surface energy of the insulating polymer to the semiconducting polymer to prevent dramatic phase separation.

heuristics.label: Build Diverse Research Groups
heuristics.summary: Build diverse research groups across different disciplines, backgrounds, and genders to fundamentally shape how complex problems are approached and solved.
heuristics.representative_quote: I think it's about the collaboration between people from different disciplines things the diversity of people coming from different background and gender

signature_quotes.label: The Vision for Flexible Electronics
signature_quotes.summary: Describing the ultimate goal of merging electronics with the human body.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Today’s electronics are rigid and brittle. We envision a future where the electronics are merged into what we wear and what we attach to our bodies or what we implant inside our bodies.

signature_quotes.label: Skin as the Ultimate Inspiration
signature_quotes.summary: Explaining the origin of her focus on electronic skin.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: So we decided to use human skin as the inspiration to define the properties and functions for the electronics we would design and build.

signature_quotes.label: No Compromise on Properties
signature_quotes.summary: Highlighting the core materials science challenge of flexible electronics.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: we had to come up with new ideas for designing semiconductors so that we do not compromise one property for another—basically to have high charge-carrier mobility while still being able to reversibly elongate the material by at least twice its original length.

signature_quotes.label: The Airplane Engine Analogy
signature_quotes.summary: Explaining the concept of Precision Health and continuous monitoring.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: imagine the engines for airplane we want to discover an issue before a catastrophic failure happens so that's exactly what we want to do for human health

signature_quotes.label: Intersection of Passion and Skill
signature_quotes.summary: Advice given to students thinking about a career in science.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: If you work at the intersection of what you are passionate about and what you are good at, you will always be successful.

signature_quotes.label: Nature's Solution to Fracture
signature_quotes.summary: Introducing the inspiration for applying self-healing chemistry to materials.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: When faced with the dilemma of mechanical fractures in biological systems, nature offers a smart solution: self-healing.

signature_quotes.label: Purpose vs. Goals
signature_quotes.summary: Explaining the difference between short-term achievements and long-term career drivers.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: purpose is different from goals goals are immediate targets we want to achieve but purpose is why we wanted to to pursue research

signature_quotes.label: Enhancing Human Function
signature_quotes.summary: Discussing the potential of high-density sensor arrays to exceed natural human capabilities.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: this is just the beginning of thinking about building electronic skin not only to mimic human function but also to enhance human function

anti_patterns.label: Relying on Rigid, Crystalline Structures
anti_patterns.summary: Assuming that high charge-carrier mobility in polymers requires a purely rigid, crystalline structure.
anti_patterns.details: While conventional wisdom dictates that rigid, crystalline structures are necessary to transport electrons effectively, this makes the material brittle and unsuitable for stretchable electronics, which require a blend of amorphous and crystalline domains.

anti_patterns.label: Using Rigid Materials for Biological Interfaces
anti_patterns.summary: Relying on rigid or high-modulus materials for implantable biological sensors or wearables.
anti_patterns.details: Rigid devices cut into soft tissue during natural movement, causing severe immune responses, scar formation, and discomfort. They are incompatible with the curved, constantly moving human body.

anti_patterns.label: Developing Materials in Isolation
anti_patterns.summary: Developing novel materials in isolation without considering the fabrication process or existing manufacturing tools.
anti_patterns.details: If new materials require entirely new manufacturing infrastructure, or if they cannot be successfully stacked and integrated into a working circuit design, the technology will struggle to reach commercialization.

anti_patterns.label: Declaring 'Failed' Experiments
anti_patterns.summary: Declaring unexpected results as a 'failed' experiment.
anti_patterns.details: It stops the inquiry process and prevents researchers from discovering incorrect assumptions that could lead to creative new breakthroughs.

anti_patterns.label: Researching to Start a Company
anti_patterns.summary: Conducting research primarily for the sake of starting a company.
anti_patterns.details: It distracts students from their primary priority: building a solid fundamental understanding and becoming the best scientist or engineer possible. True innovation naturally brings tech transfer opportunities.
anti_patterns.representative_quote: It is not a good idea to research for the sake of starting a company.

anti_patterns.label: Relying on Traditional Polymer Binders
anti_patterns.summary: Relying on traditional, rigid polymer binders for high-capacity battery electrodes.
anti_patterns.details: They lack stretchability and self-healing capabilities. When the electrode material expands and contracts, the binder permanently cracks, leading to a loss of electrical contact and rapid capacity decay.
